prompt:
We're spinning the cost-splitting logic for the group-trips feature out of the prototype. Build it as a small standalone library — plain TypeScript, node only, zero npm deps. Two entry modules; the acceptance tests import exactly these:

ledger.ts — exports `class Ledger`:
- `addMember(name)` registers a person. Blank/whitespace names → Error mentioning 'member name'; registering the same name twice → Error mentioning 'already a member'. `members()` returns the names sorted.
- `addExpense({ payer, amountCents, description?, split })` records who fronted the money and how it splits. `amountCents` must be a positive integer (RangeError otherwise — money is integer cents, never floats). The payer and every participant must be registered members, else Error `unknown member "<name>"`.
- split kinds:
  - `{ kind: 'equal', among? }` — even split across `among` (default: every member). Empty participant set → Error mentioning 'no participants'; duplicates in among → Error mentioning 'duplicate'.
  - `{ kind: 'exact', shares }` — explicit cents per person; the shares must sum to amountCents (Error mentioning 'sum' otherwise). Negative shares → RangeError; zero shares are allowed.
  - `{ kind: 'percent', shares }` — percentages that must total exactly 100 (Error mentioning '100').
  - `{ kind: 'weights', shares }` — proportional split; every weight must be a positive finite number (RangeError).
- Rounding: every booked share is integer cents. Compute the exact fractional shares, give everyone the floor, then hand the leftover cents out one each in order of largest fractional remainder, breaking ties alphabetically by name. (So 100 split equally three ways books 34/33/33 with the extra cent to the alphabetically first participant.)
- `balances()` → `[{ name, netCents }]` sorted by name, one entry per registered member, zeros included. Positive = the group owes them money. Paying credits you the full amount, participating debits your share; the column always nets to zero.

settle.ts — exports `settle(balances)` which takes exactly what `balances()` returns (rows in any order), and produces the payment plan `[{ from, to, amountCents }]`:
- reject input that doesn't sum to zero (Error mentioning 'sum to zero'); never mutate or reorder the caller's array;
- drop zero balances, then keep the plan short: FIRST pair off any debtor whose debt exactly equals some creditor's credit (walk debtors alphabetically, each takes the alphabetically first exact-matching creditor still open); THEN repeatedly have the largest remaining debtor pay the largest remaining creditor (ties on size break alphabetically). The returned list is the exact-match pairs in pairing order followed by the greedy transfers in the order they're made.

Example:

```ts
const trip = new Ledger();
for (const n of ['alex', 'blair', 'cody']) trip.addMember(n);
trip.addExpense({ payer: 'alex', amountCents: 6000, description: 'cabin', split: { kind: 'equal' } });
trip.balances(); // [{name:'alex',netCents:4000},{name:'blair',netCents:-2000},{name:'cody',netCents:-2000}]
settle(trip.balances()); // [{from:'blair',to:'alex',amountCents:2000},{from:'cody',to:'alex',amountCents:2000}]
```

Acceptance: `node --test test_ledger.ts test_settle.ts` — those two files are the contract, leave them untouched. Internal structure beyond the two entry modules is your call (the largest-remainder allocator is probably worth its own module; we'll reuse it for the tax-split work later).
